title: What is assertiveness?
citation: Alberti, R.E., & Emmons, M.L. (2008). Your perfect right: Assertiveness and equality in your life and relationships (9th ed.). Impact Publishers.
content:
Assertiveness is the ability to express your own needs, opinions, feelings, and limits clearly and respectfully, without either attacking others or abandoning yourself. It sits between two less effective patterns: passivity, in which a person consistently suppresses their own needs to avoid conflict, and aggression, in which a person pursues their own needs at the expense of others. Assertiveness is neither of these. It is an honest, direct, and considered expression of who you are and what you need.

Psychologists have long understood that assertiveness protects mental health. When people consistently fail to express their needs, resentment tends to build. Unexpressed limits are frequently crossed, not because others are unkind, but because those limits were never communicated. Over time, habitual passivity can erode self-esteem and contribute to anxiety and depression. Learning to express oneself assertively is therefore not just a social skill but a form of self-care.

It is worth being clear about what assertiveness is not. Being assertive does not mean being aggressive, demanding, or confrontational. Assertive communication is typically calm in tone, specific in content, and genuinely respectful of the other person's perspective. The goal is mutual understanding, not winning.

One practical framework for assertive communication is the DESC approach: Describe the situation factually, Express how you feel about it, Specify what you would like to be different, and consider the Consequences or benefits of that change. This structure helps keep difficult conversations grounded without sliding into blame or withdrawal.

Developing assertiveness takes practice, particularly for people who grew up in environments where self-expression was discouraged or felt unsafe. The skills can be learned gradually, starting with lower-stakes situations and building from there.
